serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of steamed Japanese rice topped with a beef hamburger patty and mentaiko sauce, as typically served at a Japanese donburi restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Beef, ground, 80% lean / 20% fat, cooked (FDC ID 174036); Rice, white, short-grain, cooked (FDC ID 169760); Pollock roe / fish roe analogue; mayonnaise and condiment components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for mentaiko beef hamburg don exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 40% steamed short-grain rice, 35% cooked beef hamburger patty (80/20 lean/fat), 10% mentaiko (spicy pollock roe, estimated from USDA fish roe data FDC ID 175168), 8% mayonnaise (FDC ID 172741), and 7% condiments/onion. All component data sourced from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and SR Legacy entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; white short-grain rice cooked GI reference; mixed beef-rice meal GI analogue
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mentaiko beef hamburg don. GI estimated at 54 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being Japanese short-grain white rice (GI ~54–56 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables). The protein and fat content from the beef patty and mentaiko sauce substantially blunt the glycemic response relative to plain rice, supporting a composite GI at the lower end of the rice reference range. GI category is low (≤55). Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as: (54 × 96.75 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 52.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from mentaiko (spicy pollock roe), soy sauce, and mayonnaise
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,395 mg per serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; portion reduction and requesting reduced-sauce preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load driven by white short-grain rice as the primary carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should be aware that a standard serving carries a glycemic load of approximately 52, primarily from white short-grain rice; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables, requesting a smaller rice portion, or substituting with brown rice where available can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef patty and cholesterol from mentaiko roe and egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should note that this dish provides an estimated 2.9 g saturated fat and 62 mg cholesterol per 100 g, with a full serving contributing approximately 13 g saturated fat; consumption should be occasional and portion-controlled in the context of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mentaiko Beef Hamburg Don is a Japanese donburi (rice bowl) dish consisting of a seasoned beef hamburger patty (hamburg steak) served over a bed of steamed Japanese short-grain rice and topped with a creamy, spicy mentaiko sauce. Mentaiko refers to spicy marinated pollock roe, a popular Japanese ingredient prized for its umami-rich, briny, and mildly spicy flavour profile. The beef patty is typically made from minced beef blended with egg, breadcrumbs, and onion, then pan-fried or grilled until cooked through with a caramelised exterior. The mentaiko sauce is commonly prepared by combining mentaiko with mayonnaise and a small amount of soy sauce or mirin, creating a rich, savoury, and slightly spicy topping that complements the beef. This dish is a fusion of the Western-influenced Japanese hamburg steak (hanbāgu) tradition with the donburi rice bowl format and the distinctly Japanese ingredient mentaiko. It is popular in Japanese casual dining restaurants and has gained traction in Singapore's Japanese food scene. Nutritionally, a standard serving provides a balanced macronutrient profile with moderate protein from beef and roe, carbohydrates from rice, and fat from the beef patty and mayonnaise-based sauce. The dish is notable for its relatively high sodium content due to the mentaiko and condiments, making it a consideration for individuals monitoring sodium intake. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low category), driven primarily by the white rice component, though the protein and fat content moderate the overall glycemic response.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for mentaiko beef hamburg don exists in any approved primary or secondary source database. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and SR Legacy data for individual components (beef patty, short-grain rice, fish roe, mayonnaise). GI is estimated by analogy to the dominant carbohydrate source (Japanese short-grain white rice) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component data used for this estimation. The food_name has been normalised to 'Mentaiko Beef Hamburg Don' removing the restaurant-specific prefix '504D'. The dish is served at GyoGyo - Funan (field survey data) at SGD 21.15. Halal status is not confirmed; mentaiko (fish roe) is permissible but the beef patty and preparation method halal status is unverified for this specific restaurant. GI category is low (GI 54 ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules). Glycemic load per serving calculated on 450 g serving size with 96.75 g available carbohydrate.
